fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out that ripple/wave click feedback thing for our component library — you know, like the material design effect where you tap a button and a circle spreads out from your finger. We talked about this in the last sprint retro. The main pain points we've been hearing from devs are: (1) they keep rewriting the same geometry math every time they need this effect on a new component, (2) the thing breaks in older app shells — I think Jess mentioned something about it not working in the legacy plugin system, same kind of fix we did for that login directive back in Q2, you can check that compatibility logic for reference — and (3) there's no consistent way to scope which elements actually respond to taps vs. which ones should be ignored (like disabled buttons). The library needs a clean adapter so we can test it from the command line by piping in JSON and reading the output. Default behavior should just work out of the box with no config needed. Visual timing, colors, and whether clicks bubble up should all be tunable. We also need to handle the case where someone wants to name their activation zones so multiple independent ripple regions don't step on each other. Please make sure the test harness writes output files per case so QA can diff them directly.

trigger_keywords: default settings when no input provided
what does an empty input return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no overrides are supplied the system must report exactly these defaults: directive=wave, color=currentColor, initialOpacity=0.2, finalOpacity=0.1, duration=0.4, dissolveDuration=0.15, easing=ease-out, cancellationPeriod=75, trigger=auto, tagName=div, disabled=false, respectDisabledAttribute=true, stopPropagation=false.

trigger_keywords: auto vs boolean vs named trigger
does a string trigger require an activator
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A trigger value of the string 'auto' is classified as kind=automatic with requiresMatchingNamedActivator=false. Boolean true or false values are classified as kind=boolean with requiresMatchingNamedActivator=false. Any other string value is classified as kind=identifier with requiresMatchingNamedActivator=true, meaning a matching named activator must exist.

trigger_keywords: what value is stored on the boundary element
how is the boundary attribute set
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the trigger is 'auto', the boundary attribute is set to the boolean true (printed as boundary=true). When the trigger is a named string identifier (not 'auto'), the boundary attribute stores that exact string value — for example trigger='stringId' produces boundary=stringId.

The relative coordinates are computed by subtracting the element box's top and left offsets from the pointer's y and x respectively: relativeX = pointer.x - elementBox.left, relativeY = pointer.y - elementBox.top. Coordinates are NOT clamped — if the pointer is outside the element boundary, the result will be negative.

trigger_keywords: how big does the ripple need to be
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system computes the Euclidean distance from the origin point to all four corners of the element rectangle (corners at (0,0), (width,0), (0,height), (width,height)). The maximum of those four distances is the required radius. The diameter is simply radius multiplied by 2. Floating-point results must be printed as raw JavaScript-precision numeric strings (e.g. radius=106.06601717798213).

trigger_keywords: what styles does the ripple wrapper get
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The generated clipping container must have a single style attribute with exactly this value: 'top: 0px; left: 0px; width: 100%; height: 100%; display: block; position: absolute; border-radius: undefined undefined undefined undefined; overflow: hidden; pointer-events: none; webkit-mask-image: -webkit-radial-gradient(white, black);'. The output prints tag, then attributes (as attributes=style=...), then style (as style=...) — both the attributes line and the style line contain the same style string.

The animated circular element is always a div. Its style is: 'position: absolute; width: {size}px; height: {size}px; top: {origin.y}px; left: {origin.x}px; border-radius: 50%; transform: translate(-50%,-50%) scale(0); transition: transform {duration}s {easing}, opacity {dissolveDuration}s {easing};'. When options are empty ({}), duration and dissolveDuration and easing are all undefined, so the style will contain 'undefineds undefined' literally. Output format mirrors the container: tag, attributes=style=..., style=....

trigger_keywords: what are the mount and update phase names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system detects two runtime families. A 'modern' runtime maps to mountPhase=mounted and updatePhase=updated. A 'legacy' runtime maps to mountPhase=inserted and updatePhase=componentUpdated. The output prints runtime (the original input value), family (the detected family label), mountPhase, and updatePhase.

trigger_keywords: where are test output files written
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes one file per case to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt — for example the first case (index 0) in feature1_default_configuration.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases goes to rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_default_configuration@000.txt. The output directory is namespaced by the cases-dir argument so different runs never overwrite each other. Each file contains only raw stdout, no pass/fail metadata.

trigger_keywords: what is the directive key called
why is it called directive not name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The top-level label for the effect type in default configuration output is 'directive' (not 'name', 'type', or 'effect'). Its default value is 'wave'. This is the first line printed when reporting the default configuration.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 (feature8_runtime_family.json): the host runtime lifecycle compatibility detection logic that maps 'modern' and 'legacy' runtime labels to their respective mount/update lifecycle phase hook names (mounted/updated vs inserted/componentUpdated).

context_pointers.vague_reference: naming your activation zones so multiple independent ripple regions don't step on each other
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 (feature3_boundary_marker.json) combined with Feature 2 (feature2_trigger_modes.json): using a named string trigger (kind=identifier, requiresMatchingNamedActivator=true) which stores the identifier as the boundary value, allowing targeted activation of specific boundary elements.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Could you check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the feature descriptions in start.md? Those should have the exact expected input/output behavior you're looking for. If it's about visual defaults or timing values, the feature1 default config case is usually the best reference.

feature1_default_configuration.json.description: Reports the complete built-in configuration used when no caller overrides are supplied.

cases.expected_output:
directive=wave
color=currentColor
initialOpacity=0.2
finalOpacity=0.1
duration=0.4
dissolveDuration=0.15
easing=ease-out
cancellationPeriod=75
trigger=auto
tagName=div
disabled=false
respectDisabledAttribute=true
stopPropagation=false

feature2_trigger_modes.json.description: Classifies activation trigger settings as automatic behavior, boolean behavior, or a named identifier that requires a matching activator.

feature3_boundary_marker.json.description: Marks an effect boundary so ordinary activation uses a generic marker and named activation stores the requested identifier.

feature4_relative_pointer.json.description: Converts a page pointer position into coordinates relative to the decorated element's top-left corner, preserving negative coordinates outside the element.

feature5_coverage_radius.json.description: Calculates the ripple radius needed for an origin point to reach the farthest corner of an element rectangle.

feature6_container_element.json.description: Creates the clipping container for the visual effect using the requested tag and fixed positioning and masking styles.

feature7_wave_element.json.description: Creates the animated circular visual element at the requested origin and size, applying the supplied visual timing options as inline styles.

feature8_runtime_family.json.description: Detects whether the host UI runtime uses the modern or legacy plugin shape and reports the lifecycle phase names required by that runtime.
